name: Joyous Garde
description: Joyous Garde, later known as Dolorous Garde, is a castle prominent in Arthurian legend, most notably associated with Sir Lancelot. Initially a fortress of great beauty and happiness, it served as Lancelot's primary stronghold after he conquered it. It's a place of both triumph and tragedy, representing Lancelot's prowess and chivalry, but also the events that led to the downfall of Camelot. The castle witnessed numerous battles and served as a refuge for Lancelot and his followers. After Lancelot's death, the castle became associated with sorrow, reflecting the decline of the Arthurian world. Legend holds that Queen Guinevere was also held here for a time. The very name reflects the duality of the Arthurian cycle, a place of joy turned to sorrow through betrayal and conflict. Its physical location varies depending on the source, sometimes placed in Northumbria or Northumberland, but its symbolic importance remains consistent throughout the tales, representing the rise and fall of Arthurian ideals and the complex relationships between its key figures.
subtitle: Lancelot's Fortress: Joy and Sorrow

keyMyths.title: Lancelot Takes Joyous Garde
keyMyths.description: Lancelot, through his bravery and skill, conquers the castle, renaming it Joyous Garde as a symbol of his victory. It becomes his primary residence and a symbol of his power.

keyMyths.title: Guinevere's Imprisonment
keyMyths.description: Queen Guinevere is sometimes held captive at Joyous Garde after being rescued by Lancelot. This event highlights the complexities of their relationship and the political turmoil of the time.

sources.author: Sir Thomas Malory
sources.description: A foundational text of Arthurian literature that features Joyous Garde prominently.
sources.text: A foundational text of Arthurian literature that features Joyous Garde prominently.

sources.author: Chrétien de Troyes
sources.work: Lancelot, the Knight of the Cart
sources.description: An early Arthurian romance that contributes to the legend of Lancelot and his association with castles.
sources.text: An early Arthurian romance that contributes to the legend of Lancelot and his association with castles.

worshipPractices.name: Chivalric Observances
worshipPractices.description: Joyous Garde, under Lancelot's rule, emphasized the knightly virtues of chivalry. Daily life involved upholding honor, protecting the weak, and demonstrating martial prowess. These virtues were seen as a form of service to God and the realm.

worshipPractices.name: Courtly Love Rituals
worshipPractices.description: The culture of Joyous Garde celebrated courtly love, particularly the idealized adoration of a noble lady. Knights would often dedicate their deeds and victories to their chosen lady, engaging in elaborate displays of affection and devotion, seeing it as a way to refine their character.

festivals.name: Tournaments of Joyous Garde
festivals.timing: Held annually, usually in the spring or summer.
festivals.description: Grand tournaments were organized to showcase the skill and valor of the knights. These events included jousting, melee combat, archery, and feasting, attracting participants and spectators from across the land and solidifying the castle's reputation for martial excellence.

festivals.name: Feasts of the Round Table
festivals.timing: Celebrated on significant anniversaries and victories.
festivals.description: Joyous Garde hosted feasts in the tradition of the Round Table, emphasizing camaraderie and fellowship among the knights. These gatherings involved storytelling, music, dancing, and the sharing of news and adventures, reinforcing the bonds of the Arthurian brotherhood.

cultural.modernLegacy: Joyous Garde, as a symbol of both knightly perfection and tragic downfall due to human flaws, influences modern literature and art by exploring themes of chivalry, honor, and the consequences of forbidden love. It appears in numerous adaptations of Arthurian legends, often representing a lost ideal or a reminder of the fragility of Camelot's dream.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Valhalla, like Joyous Garde, is a renowned hall associated with great warriors and victory. Both locations represent a place of honor and strength, though Valhalla is explicitly for the dead.

Mount Olympus, the home of the Olympian gods, mirrors Joyous Garde as a powerful and significant location. Both places are central to their respective mythologies and symbolize authority and importance.

Ryūgū-jō, the Dragon Palace, is a magical underwater kingdom, similar to the idealized image of Joyous Garde before its fall. Both places are fantastical and represent a departure from the mundane world.

Tír na nÓg, the Land of Eternal Youth, shares the concept of a beautiful and idyllic location with Joyous Garde's initial state. Both are places of enhanced existence, though Tír na nÓg focuses on immortality and beauty rather than martial prowess.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White symbolizes purity and the initial noble intentions Lancelot had for Joyous Garde, representing a fresh start.

associations.name: Chivalry
associations.significance: Joyous Garde embodies the ideals of chivalry, being a place where knights sought refuge and upheld their oaths, especially under Lancelot's rule.

associations.name: Stone
associations.significance: Stone represents the enduring strength and resilience of Joyous Garde, a lasting monument to both Lancelot's achievements and eventual downfall.

associations.name: Rose
associations.significance: The rose symbolizes beauty and love, reflecting the initial joyous atmosphere and Lancelot's romantic involvement within the castle walls.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: The sun represents the initial prosperity and radiance of Joyous Garde, signifying a period of glory and happiness under Lancelot's command.

associations.name: Lion
associations.significance: The lion symbolizes courage and nobility, reflecting Lancelot's character and his role as the lord of Joyous Garde.

associations.name: Tragedy
associations.significance: Joyous Garde's transformation to Dolorous Garde represents the inevitable sorrow and loss that followed Lancelot, turning a place of joy into one of grief.

companions.name: Sir Lancelot
companions.description: Lancelot conquered Joyous Garde and made it his primary stronghold, becoming its protector and lord. His presence defined the castle's period of greatest renown.

companions.name: Sir Lavaine
companions.description: Lavaine was a loyal knight and constant companion to Lancelot, likely spending time at Joyous Garde. He was known for his courage and unwavering devotion.

companions.name: Elaine of Corbenic
companions.description: Elaine, mother of Galahad, had a complex relationship with Lancelot and thus a connection to Joyous Garde. Her presence, even indirectly, influenced events at the castle.

companions.name: Knights of the Round Table
companions.description: As a prominent knight, Lancelot's allies from the Round Table would have visited and potentially defended Joyous Garde. They would have been welcomed as honored guests.
